title: On Eisenstein’s Jugendtraum for Complex Cubic Fields
speaker: Pierre Charollois
affiliation: Institut de mathématiques de Jussieu-PRG, Sorbonne Université
abstract: In the early 2000’s Ruijsenaars and Felder-Varchenko have introduced the elliptic gamma function, a remarkable multivariable meromorphic q-series that comes from mathematical physics. It satisfies modular functional equations under the group SL3(Z) which make it a higher dimensional analogue of the Jacobi theta function. In this work, we unveil the place that this function and its avatars play in number theory. Our main thesis is that these functions play the role of modular units in extending the theory of complex multiplication to complex cubic fields.

proof_stages.label: Hilbert-12 and classical explicit-unit template
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls Hilbert’s 12th problem and reviews the rational and imaginary-quadratic cases, where cyclotomic, modular, and elliptic units arise from special functions with transformation and distribution laws and are tied to partial zeta derivatives by Kronecker limit formulas.

proof_stages.label: Complex cubic target and Eisenstein motivation
proof_stages.summary: After noting other approaches to Hilbert 12, the speaker fixes a complex cubic field, introduces Rains’s elliptic gamma function as a substitute for impossible triply periodic meromorphic functions, and motivates it historically through Eisenstein’s proposed triple products with inequalities.

proof_stages.label: Eisenstein smoothing yields elliptic-gamma ratios
proof_stages.summary: Following Eisenstein’s two prescriptions—take quotients of quotients and impose inequalities—the speaker smooths triple products, performs the one-dimensional product first, and obtains ratios of elliptic gamma functions.

proof_stages.label: Prototype cubic computation and Galois pattern
proof_stages.summary: A live computation for a complex cubic field shows that a smoothed elliptic-gamma value is numerically recognized as a unit in a field with the expected ramification, and that many conjugates are given by analogous gamma ratios.

proof_stages.label: SL3 elliptic-gamma cocycle and arithmetic setup
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states the Felder–Varchenko/Rains family of functions indexed by primitive vectors in Z^3, their periodicity, equivariance, and one-cocycle relation under SL3(Z), then adapts this to a complex cubic field using a conductor, fractional ideals, a fundamental unit, and an adapted basis.

proof_stages.label: General algebraicity and reciprocity conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The arithmetic invariant is defined as a smoothed value of gamma_{a,epsilon a} at the embedded lattice basis, and the main conjecture asserts independence of choices, algebraicity as a unit in a specified ray class field, unit-circle behavior of real-place conjugates, and a reciprocity law.

proof_stages.label: Kronecker-limit formula and Stark compatibility
proof_stages.summary: A theorem identifies the derivative at zero of a smoothed partial zeta combination with the logarithm of the absolute value of the gamma invariant, via nonstandard GL3 Eisenstein series and deformation to the boundary, thereby making the conjecture a refinement compatible with Stark’s conjectures.

proof_stages.label: ATR and higher-dimensional outlook
proof_stages.summary: The construction is placed in the broader Hilbert-12 setting for ATR fields, compared with Barnes-gamma and Hilbert-modular approaches, and extended conjecturally toward SL4 and SLn multivariable elliptic gamma functions evaluated on higher-rank tori.

examples.name: Cyclotomic unit 1 - exp(2πia/f)
examples.note: The speaker uses this standard unit in a cyclotomic field to make concrete the kind of algebraic units sought in explicit class field theory.

examples.name: CM numerical example over Q(i)
examples.note: A theta-ratio evaluated at CM/torsion data is numerically recognized by LLL as an algebraic unit, illustrating the classical elliptic-unit template rather than generating the new construction.
examples.engagement_reason: he runs a numerical CM example over Q(i), evaluates a theta ratio, recognizes it as a root of a polynomial, and checks discriminant ramification

examples.name: Complex cubic field Q(∛7)
examples.note: The live computation of a smoothed elliptic-gamma ratio for the complex cubic field generated by a cubic root of 7 gives numerical evidence for algebraicity, unit behavior, and prescribed ramification in the conjecture.
examples.engagement_reason: he computes the elliptic-gamma ratio for Q(∛7), identifies a degree-18 polynomial/unit, discusses ramification, and describes further roots via gamma values

examples.name: Barnes double gamma comparison
examples.note: In response to an audience question, the Barnes gamma functions are compared with the elliptic gamma as a related special-function construction, clarifying why a cone containing a lattice line gives the modularity needed here.
examples.engagement_reason: he only compares the construction to Barnes double gamma functions by describing cone index sets and modularity differences, without carrying out a concrete computation on them
